title: Firms allowed to hire immigrant manual laborers expand to hire more Americans
gist: Using the H-2B visa lottery as a natural experiment across 472 firms, Clemens and Lewis find that firms randomly allowed to hire low-skill immigrant labor grow revenue 12-14 percent and increase (not reduce) their employment of Americans, because the scale effect of immigrant labor outweighs the substitution effect by more than 2 to 1. They argue the Trump administration's 2026 maximalist restrictions on lawful immigration will shrink firms and become a macroeconomic drag rather than protect native workers.

insights.claim: The US government in 2026 enacted the largest shift in immigration policy since the 1924 quota act, and the Trump administration has chosen maximalist restrictions on lawful immigration rather than opening offsetting lawful channels.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens,Ethan G. Lewis
insights.so_what: A structural regime change in labor supply is underway; sectors dependent on foreign labor face a durable, policy-driven supply shock worth pricing into labor-cost and wage forecasts.
insights.quote: The US government has recently enacted the largest shift in immigration policy since 1924

insights.claim: The 2026 restrictions cut, penalized, or suspended lawful admissions of refugees, students, high-skill workers, and self-sponsored workers, barred most or all visas for 39 countries, and reduced authorized immigration by more than unauthorized immigration.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens,Ethan G. Lewis
insights.so_what: The cut is falling hardest on legal, high-productivity immigration channels, implying broader growth drag than the border-focused headlines suggest.
insights.quote: They have cut authorized immigration by more than unauthorized immigration
insights.locator: So far, Trump administration officials have chosen maximalist restrictions

insights.claim: American economic demand for immigrant labor remains strong in construction, care work, and food production, where lawful immigrant workers increase the supply of key services, spark growth, and reduce inflation.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens,Ethan G. Lewis
insights.so_what: Cutting these workers is disinflation-in-reverse; watch construction, elder/child care, and food-processing wage and price indices as leading indicators of restriction-driven cost pressure.
insights.locator: Yet Americans' economic demand for immigrant labor remains strong

themes.theme: H-2B lottery as a natural experiment

insights.claim: Because Department of Labor demand for the H-2B visa exceeds the congressional quota, scarce slots are allocated by random lottery, creating a de facto randomized experiment in which similar firms are randomly authorized or denied the right to hire foreign manual workers.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens,Ethan G. Lewis
insights.so_what: Random assignment removes selection bias that plagues most immigration studies, making these causal estimates far more credible than typical labor-econ correlations analysts cite.
insights.quote: the Department of Labor distributes the scarce slots the way a stadium handles oversubscribed tickets: by lottery
insights.locator: A lottery for the right to hire

insights.claim: The H-2B visa is the largest US visa for nonfarm, low-skill labor, used by landscaping crews, hotels, seafood processors, carnivals, and forestry contractors, and it legalizes a multibillion-dollar annual industry in US imports of fundamental services.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens,Ethan G. Lewis
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific labor-intensive service sub-sectors most exposed to H-2B quota policy for screening equity and credit exposure.
insights.locator: A lottery for the right to hire

insights.claim: The authors surveyed 472 winning and losing firms from the 2021 and 2022 lotteries and pre-registered their analysis methods online before collecting data, so findings could not be bent to preconceptions; losing the lottery cut a firm's foreign-labor employment by roughly half.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens,Ethan G. Lewis
insights.so_what: Pre-registration plus a ~50 percent first-stage treatment effect makes the identification strong; a credible evidentiary base for arguing quota expansion, not just advocacy.
insights.quote: We had irreversibly posted our analysis methods online before we collected the data, so the findings could not be bent to fit preconceptions.
insights.locator: That presents a rare opportunity to isolate the pure effects of foreign workers

themes.theme: Banana vs pasta: substitution vs scale framework

insights.claim: The common fear that immigrants displace native workers rests on a 'banana model' in which labor is just more sellers in a market, so more immigrant labor must eventually push out native labor.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens,Ethan G. Lewis
insights.so_what: Names the intuitive but incomplete mental model driving restrictionist policy; recognizing it as a partial-equilibrium error is the crux of the contrarian trade.
insights.quote: If immigrants in an economic sense were just more people selling labor, by the same logic they must displace native workers sooner or later.
insights.locator: Call the simple picture the banana model

insights.claim: Treating immigrant and native labor as complementary factors of production, the 'pasta model' shows that restricting one factor triggers both a substitution effect (swap toward native labor) and a scale effect (shrink total production), so the net effect on native-labor demand can go either way and must be measured empirically.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens,Ethan G. Lewis
insights.so_what: A rigorous mental model: whether immigration helps or hurts native jobs is an empirical question about task complementarity, not ideology, so it varies by sector and era.
insights.quote: restricting one key factor of production (immigrant labor) can either reduce or raise firms' demand for another key factor of production (native labor)
insights.locator: Immigrant labor is a factor of production: Thinking about bananas and pasta

insights.claim: Whether native and immigrant workers are net substitutes or complements turns on how much they specialize in different, complementary tasks such as cooking versus serving or construction versus architecture.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens,Ethan G. Lewis
insights.so_what: Task-specialization is the observable mechanism; sectors where immigrant and native roles are distinct will see scale effects dominate and suffer most from restriction.

insights.claim: Firms randomly allowed to hire H-2B workers produced more, with revenue rising 12 to 14 percent, higher profit growth, and more reinvestment, inconsistent with foreign workers merely pushing out natives doing the same tasks.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens,Ethan G. Lewis
insights.so_what: A 12-14 percent revenue lift from visa access quantifies the firm-level cost of quota denial; H-2B-dependent private businesses face material growth and margin risk under restriction.
insights.quote: Firms randomly allowed to hire H-2B workers produced more, with revenue rising 12 to 14 percent.

insights.claim: For a 10 percent increase in a firm's H-2B hiring, the substitution channel would trim low-skill US employment by about 0.6 percent, but the scale channel raises US employment by about 1.4 percent, for a net positive of roughly 0.9 percent.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens,Ethan G. Lewis
insights.so_what: The scale effect is more than twice the substitution effect, so at the margin immigrant hires create net American jobs; the empirical decomposition directly rebuts the displacement thesis.
insights.quote: The scale effect is more than twice the substitution effect, so the net change in US jobs is positive, about 0.9 percent.
insights.locator: The figure above shows the two competing effects

insights.claim: Across all firms, randomly gaining the chance to hire foreign workers did not reduce US hiring and if anything raised US employment, with the gains concentrated in rural areas where employers had the fewest labor alternatives.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens,Ethan G. Lewis
insights.so_what: Directly contradicts the 'immigrants take American jobs' consensus in exactly the low-skill setting where displacement fears are strongest; rural labor markets are most exposed to restriction damage.
insights.quote: If anything, US employment rose, particularly in rural areas where employers had the fewest alternatives.

insights.claim: An independent research team led by Catalina Amuedo-Dorantes, using the full universe of H-2B employers' administrative data, reached broadly the same conclusion that restricting low-wage foreign labor shrinks the firms that use it without protecting existing workers.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens,Ethan G. Lewis
insights.so_what: Independent replication on full administrative data raises confidence that the result generalizes beyond the 472-firm survey; the finding is robust, not a sampling artifact.
insights.quote: Restricting low-wage foreign labor shrinks the firms that use it without protecting the workers already there.
insights.locator: A skeptic might ask whether the 472 firms we surveyed are representative

insights.claim: Expanding lawful low-skill immigration channels would increase US economic growth and native job creation, while the current sharp turn toward immigrant exclusion will become a macroeconomic drag.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens,Ethan G. Lewis
insights.so_what: Frames immigration restriction as a headwind to US GDP and employment; a bearish input for growth-sensitive positioning and a watch item for 2026-27 labor-market softness.
insights.quote: Without that critical step, the US government's current sharp turn toward immigrant exclusion will become a macroeconomic drag.

insights.claim: Immigrant removals and militarized raids are already depressing US job creation in industries where immigrants fill key roles.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens,Ethan G. Lewis
insights.so_what: Enforcement actions are a real-time negative shock to sector employment; monitor construction, agriculture, hospitality, and food-processing payrolls for early evidence of the drag.
insights.quote: Immigrant removals and militarized raids are already depressing US job creation in industries where immigrants fill key roles.
